data.title: Nike slides 9% after issuing soft guidance during its earnings call
data.description:
Nike (NKE) fell sharply in postmarket trading on Tuesday after issuing soft guidance on its earnings conference call.

The company expects sales to decline between 2% and 4% in FQ4 to miss the consensus expectation for an increase of 1.9%.  Notably, Nike (NKE) sees a 20% decline in revenue for the Greater China business.

CFO Matt Friend said the company recognizes that the environment has become increasingly dynamic and warned it could experience unplanned volatility due to the disruption in the Middle East, rising oil prices, and other factors that could impact either input costs or consumer behavior. Meanwhile, CFO Elliott Hill acknowledged the company's comeback is taking longer than he would like but thinks the current strategy will pay off in the long term.

Shares of Nike (NKE) moved down 9.1% in postmarket trading after moving 3.1% higher during the regular session.

data.title: Nike anticipates Q4 revenue down 2% to 4% as it targets finishing 'Win Now' actions by year-end
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NIKE, Inc. (NKE) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "Last quarter, we said we were in the middle innings of our comeback. Since then, we have continued to take meaningful actions to improve the health, quality and foundation of our business." (CEO, President & Director Elliott Hill)

  • "One of the most important actions we took this quarter was further removing unhealthy inventory of our classic footwear franchises from the marketplace. That created roughly a 5-point headwind to our reported results. It was intentional. It was necessary." (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • "NIKE Running up over 20% for the quarter." (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • "Our new Nike Mind platform with over 150 patents filed globally was the highlight of the quarter... The Mind 001 sold out in all geographies." (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • "We responded by doubling production of NIKE Mind over the next 2 seasons to meet demand from more than 2 million consumers who signed up for notify me on NIKE.com." (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • "You saw the early signs of those actions this quarter, and it is a critical step in our return to double-digit EBIT margins." (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • "For this quarter, revenues were flat on a reported basis and down 3% on a currency-neutral basis." (Executive VP & CFO Matthew Friend)

  • "Gross margins declined 130 basis points to 40.2% on a reported basis, primarily due to 300 basis points associated with higher tariffs in North America." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "Let me provide some additional context on the $230 million charge we incurred this quarter due to employee-related severance costs primarily in supply chain and technology." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

Outlook

  • "We expect revenues in Q4 to be down 2% to 4%." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We expect Greater China to be down approximately 20% in the fourth quarter, reflecting reduced sell-in... as well as accelerated actions to clean up the marketplace." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We expect sequential improvement in gross margin with Q4 down approximately 25 to 75 basis points, including 250 basis points due to higher tariffs in North America." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We expect Q4 SG&A dollars to be flat to down slightly." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We expect other expense net of interest income to be an expense of $15 million to $25 million in the fourth quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We will return to providing full year and long-term guidance at our Investor Day in the fall." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • Change from last quarter’s framing: in Q2, management emphasized a 90-day guidance approach ("we've been providing 90 days of guidance for the last 5 quarters") while in Q3 it added a broader view ("provide greater visibility to how we see the business trend from here through the end of this calendar year"). (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

Financial Results

  • Revenue of $11,229,428,660.0 vs. $11,229,428,660.0 (analysts estimate for fiscal Q3 2026). EPS of $0.35 vs. $0.28 (analysts estimate for fiscal Q3 2026).

  • "NIKE Direct was down 7% with NIKE Digital declining 9% and NIKE stores down 5%. Wholesale grew 1%." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "SG&A was up 2% on a reported basis versus the prior year due to employee severance charges we incurred in the quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "Our effective tax rate was 20%. Earnings per share was $0.35 and inventory decreased 1% versus the prior year with units down mid-single digits." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We continue to evaluate opportunities related to supply chain which could result in additional financial impacts in future quarters though we believe the actions taken this quarter will represent the largest financial impact." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

Q&A

  • Lorraine Maikis, BofA Securities: asked how EMEA issues are diagnosed and fixed; Executive VP & CFO Friend said EMEA is seeing "growth in performance" but "sell-through trends are not yet where we want them to be," pointing to macro/traffic pressures and promotional activity, and added it expects to exit Q4 with "elevated inventory in EMEA" while working through actions; CEO, President & Director Hill added "we do have a new leader in place" and said he has "tremendous confidence in César Garcia" and the focus is assortments, presentation, and sell-through with strategic partners.

  • Adrienne Yih-Tennant, Barclays: pressed on the calendar-year guidance shaping and what “flattish” earnings covers; Executive VP & CFO Friend said the guidance is meant to "provid[e] transparency... over the next 9 months" aligned to completing actions by year-end, with North America momentum offset by Greater China headwinds from reduced sell-in, and emphasized "we expect margins to inflect in Q2."

  • Simeon Siegel, Guggenheim: asked about DTC quality and severance in overhead; CEO, President & Director Hill said Nike is shifting from "a NIKE Direct first offense" to "a balanced and integrated marketplace"; Executive VP & CFO Friend pointed to North America digital improvement with better launches and "continued improvement in average retail discounts," plus "demand on the Nike App grow in Q3 in North America" and "positive growth in all channels... for the first time in 2 years."

  • Michael Binetti, Evercore ISI: asked about Q4 puts/takes in EMEA and strong EBIT despite revenue decline; Executive VP & CFO Friend said the change was Sportswear sell-through and guidance reflects careful marketplace management, also factoring "traffic" disruption in the Middle East.

  • Brooke Roach, Goldman Sachs: asked on stabilizing Sportswear and more reset in Classics; CEO, President & Director Hill said "the Air Force 1 and the AJ1, they stabilized this quarter" with "month-to-month improvement in full price realization" and noted "We're still stabilizing the Dunks," while citing strong launches (AJ 11 Gamma, AJ5 Wolf Grey, Air Max 95) with "high full price realization and really strong sell-through."

  • Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer: asked why the turnaround is taking longer (internal vs. external); CEO, President & Director Hill said "it's a little bit of both" and highlighted differing starting points by geography and marketplace structures.

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: asked about global sportswear backdrop and parsing "self-inflicted" headwinds and sell-through; CEO, President & Director Hill said sportswear remains "critical" and Nike is taking a "streets-up approach"; Executive VP & CFO Friend said to use the Q4 revenue range as a guide for the next two quarters and described continued cleaning actions in EMEA/APLA and managed sell-in in Greater China to drive "sequential improvement of sell-through rates."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone: slightly negative to pressing, with repeated probing on EMEA underperformance, guidance shape/“flattish” earnings, DTC quality vs. wholesale, and the duration/causes of the turnaround (e.g., "the process is taking longer than you initially expected"). (Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer)

  • Management tone: slightly positive but candid about near-term pressure, repeatedly emphasizing deliberate actions and time required ("we are not satisfied" and "parts of it are taking longer than I'd like") while pointing to confidence markers such as "I am confident" and "we remain confident" tied to stated priorities and timelines. (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • Versus last quarter: management continued the "middle innings" framing but added more explicit near-term pressure drivers in Q3 (inventory removal headwind and severance), while Q2 leaned more heavily on "slightly better than we had anticipated" and momentum signals like wholesale growth and improving order books. (CEO, President & Director Hill)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance approach broadened in Q3: Q2 focused on Q3 guidance ("We expect Q3 revenues to be down low single digits") while Q3 added a calendar-year trend view ("provide greater visibility") and specific Q4 ranges ("down 2% to 4%") alongside a commitment to restore longer-term guidance at an Investor Day.

  • Turnaround actions shifted from leadership/structure emphasis to execution and cost reset: Q2 highlighted leadership changes ("all geographies will now report directly to me" and naming a COO) while Q3 emphasized additional marketplace cleanup ("further removing unhealthy inventory") and a cost-base reset including "the $230 million charge" and supply chain/technology actions.

  • Geographic narrative changed in EMEA: Q2 described EMEA as maintaining "a healthy marketplace" despite promotional activity, while Q3 described sharper pressure from Sportswear sell-through gaps, higher promotions, Middle East disruption, and an expectation to exit Q4 with "elevated inventory" in EMEA.

  • North America signals evolved: Q2 highlighted strong wholesale growth and digital premium repositioning; Q3 emphasized accelerating wholesale momentum, improving sell-through (not yet at target), and the first time in "2 years" of positive growth in all channels in North America.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Digital is still too promotional. Markdowns across the marketplace remain elevated." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We anticipate adding the fourth quarter with elevated inventory" in EMEA amid a "highly promotional marketplace" and "recent disruption in the Middle East." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We expect these actions will continue throughout fiscal '27 and remain a headwind to revenue growth" in Greater China as Nike reduces sell-in and cleans the marketplace. (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We also recognize that the environment around us has become increasingly dynamic, and could experience unplanned volatility due to the disruption in the Middle East, rising oil prices and other factors that could impact either input costs or consumer behavior." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q3 as a quarter of deliberate cleanup and restructuring: CEO Elliott Hill said Nike intentionally removed unhealthy classic footwear inventory, creating a roughly 5-point headwind, while pointing to faster-moving proof points including Running up over 20% and Nike Mind selling out. CFO Matt Friend reported flat revenue and $0.35 EPS, alongside a $230 million severance charge tied to resetting the cost base in supply chain and technology. For Q4, management guided revenue down 2% to 4% and said Greater China is expected to be down about 20%, while positioning gross margin to begin inflecting after tariff pressure eases and “Win Now” actions near completion by the end of the calendar year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Edible Garden outlines RTD expansion with plans to be in market toward tail end of 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Edible Garden AG Incorporated (EDBL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • “2025 was a defining year for Enable Garden as we continue to build on our foundation and expand our long-term growth potential,” said Founder, CEO, President, Treasurer, Secretary & Chairman James Kras, describing “a deliberate strategy to grow beyond our core controlled environment agriculture platform into a broader innovation-driven consumer packaged goods business focusing on higher growth, higher margin opportunities.”
  • Kras said the company “secur[ed] new and expanded placements with key retail partners, including Kroger, Weis Markets, Safeway, The Fresh Market and Busch's, increasing our distribution to nearly 6,000 store locations,” and added that these efforts and “targeted investments in customer onboarding… resulted in incremental distribution of more than 700 additional retail locations.”
  • Kras highlighted category performance, citing “double-digit growth in cut herbs,” “continued strength in our vitamin and supplement portfolio,” and “significant growth in our condiment platform, supported by new customer wins such as Wakefern and Safeway.”
  • On strategy, Kras said, “Key next step in our strategy is expanding into the ready-to-drink, or RTD category,” adding that the company “announced the development of a state-of-the-art RTD manufacturing initiative at our Midwest facility” and “selected Tetra Pak… to plan, install and integrate proprietary processing capabilities.”
  • “Revenue for the 3 months ended December 31, 2025, was approximately $4.1 million compared to $3.9 million in the prior year period,” said Interim Chief Financial Officer Kostas Dafoulas, who also said Q4 cost pressures reflected onboarding investments and that “we expect the cost structure to normalize as those programs mature and volume increases.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide formal 2026 revenue or EPS guidance in this transcript.
  • Dafoulas provided margin and growth framework tied to portfolio mix, saying the core CEA business could “return to steady growth in the high single digits sort of range,” nutraceuticals showed “double-digit, 20%-ish range year-over-year” growth, and “blended margin kind of low double digits to mid-teens is a reasonable expectation going forward.”
  • For RTD, Dafoulas described “margins kind of in the 20% to 30% range” as “first expectations” while the company scopes inputs and project details.
  • On the RTD timeline, Kras said, “our plan is to really hopefully be out in the marketplace probably towards the tail end of 2027.”

Financial Results

  • Dafoulas reported Q4 revenue of “approximately $4.1 million” and said the quarter included “our first international CPG segment of Kick. Sports Nutrition to PriceSmart.”
  • Dafoulas said Q4 cost of goods sold was “approximately $5.3 million,” and gross profit was “approximately a $1.2 million loss,” attributing the quarter to “a deliberate decision to absorb elevated costs to secure a 2026 shelf space.”
  • Dafoulas reported Q4 SG&A of “approximately $4.6 million,” citing “depreciation and rent tied to the NaturalShrimp asset acquisition,” “higher legal and professional fees,” “capital markets activities,” and “higher compensation expenses,” and added that “a meaningful portion reflects nonrecurring or deal-related costs rather than ongoing run rate expense.”
  • For the full year, Dafoulas reported revenue of “approximately $12.8 million versus $13.9 million in 2024,” stating “the headline decline is largely a function of our strategic exit from floral and lettuce.”
  • Dafoulas reported full-year cost of goods sold of “approximately $13 million,” full-year gross profit of “approximately a loss of $0.2 million,” and said “gross margin recovery is a top priority for 2026.”
  • Dafoulas reported full-year SG&A of “approximately $15.3 million versus $11.6 million in 2024,” and said “stockholders' equity improved through the preferred stock issuance associated with the NaturalShrimp acquisition” while “total debt declined approximately $0.6 million year-over-year.”

Q&A

  • Jeremy Pearlman, Maxim Group: asked how to view margins shifting from fresh to CPG/RTD and the CPG vs. fresh breakdown into 2026; CEO Kras said shelf-stable RTD/CPG should have “much more of a robust margin” and “a much more stable business,” and CFO Dafoulas added blended margin expectations of “low double digits to mid-teens” with RTD “margins kind of in the 20% to 30% range.”
  • Jeremy Pearlman, Maxim Group: asked where the company will compete in RTD and whether it will be produced at the Midwest facility; CEO Kras said RTD will be “primarily in the protein segment,” spanning “sports nutrition, performance nutrition,” including “our own Kick” and “a GLP-1… under our Jealousy brand,” and also “private label,” adding, “our goal is… to sell out the plant in the next 90 days or so.”
  • Jeremy Pearlman, Maxim Group: asked about CapEx, build-out timeline, and scale for the Midwest facility; CEO Kras said, “I don't want to give any specific numbers,” but described “some real CapEx” and said the company is working on “incentives,” with a target to be “out in the marketplace… towards the tail end of 2027.”
  • Nick Pincus, Forest Capital: asked about sustainability of distribution gains to “nearly 6,000 locations”; CEO Kras said exits from “floral and lettuce” helped focus the business, cited “good same-store sales,” and said, “you're going to see more store count… across the whole business,” including RTD.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with questions focused on margin structure, revenue mix visibility, and RTD execution; Pearlman called RTD “a really great opportunity for the company” while pressing for CapEx and timelines.
  • Management: positive and expansion-focused in prepared remarks and Q&A; Kras called RTD “transformative for Edible Garden” and Dafoulas emphasized normalization and recovery, including “we expect the cost structure to normalize” and “gross margin recovery is a top priority for 2026.”
  • Versus Q3 2025, the current call leaned more heavily into RTD manufacturing plans and timeline language, while Q3 emphasized the shift toward shelf-stable CPG and retail footprint expansion.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The most notable shift from Q3 to Q4 was the move from discussing the NaturalShrimp/Midwest facility as an R&D and product hub to positioning it as an RTD manufacturing initiative with Tetra Pak integration and a stated target to be in market “towards the tail end of 2027.”
  • Q4 emphasized distribution scale (“nearly 6,000 store locations”) and onboarding-driven cost pressure, while Q3 emphasized that shelf-stable portfolio growth was “54% year-over-year in Q3” and detailed cash and net loss figures not repeated in the Q4 prepared financial remarks.
  • Q&A focus moved from Q3 questions about initial facility utilization and private label/margin mechanics to Q4 questions centered on RTD category focus, expected margins, and CapEx/timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Dafoulas flagged near-term cost and margin pressure from onboarding, saying Q4 costs reflected onboarding “during a seasonally compressed period,” and gross profit was impacted by a “deliberate decision to absorb elevated costs to secure a 2026 shelf space.”
  • Kras declined to provide RTD CapEx specifics, saying, “I don't want to give any specific numbers,” while acknowledging the need to “buy machines and retrofit a building” and describing it as “some real CapEx.”
  • Dafoulas said part of the nutraceutical trade-off is margin, noting co-manufacturing means “the margins are not as rich as if we were to do it ourselves.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q4 2025 as a quarter of expanded national distribution and deliberate, front-loaded onboarding investment that pressured gross profit, while reiterating that margin recovery is a stated 2026 priority. The call’s central strategic update was the RTD initiative at the Midwest facility with Tetra Pak integration, with management positioning RTD and broader shelf-stable categories as the path to a more scalable, higher-margin mix and targeting commercial presence “towards the tail end of 2027.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nano Dimension forecasts $130M-$140M 2026 revenue as board targets Q2 path to maximize shareholder value
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nano Dimension (NNDM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Stehlin said the company “streamlined operations, reduced cash burn and aligned resources around forward leading industries,” and added it “provided financial guidance for the first time in recent history and exceeded our fourth quarter expectations.”
  • Stehlin said Nano Dimension repurchased “more than 14.4 million shares in the last 3.5 months of the year because we believe that our stock is undervalued,” while also emphasizing ongoing cost actions: “we're continuing to reduce expenses... by eliminating costs in areas where we do not see long-term value.”
  • Stehlin highlighted demand and customer activity across “advanced electronics, aerospace, automotive, defense, food and beverage and next-generation computing infrastructure,” and said Q4 momentum was “generally broad-based.”
  • Stehlin discussed the strategic alternatives process, saying communications were “intentional,” and that the company “expect[s] that in the second quarter, we will make a series of announcements that will make clear our path forward to maximizing shareholder value in a relatively short period of time.”
  • CFO John Brenton said: “Revenue for the fourth quarter was $35.3 million,” and added that “we delivered results that exceeded the financial guidance we outlined on our third quarter call, reflecting improved execution, stronger demand across our priority industry segments and disciplined cost management.”

Outlook

  • With no usable analysts estimates provided, the transcript did not include a comparison to Street expectations.
  • Brenton guided 2026 revenue to “the range of $130 million to $140 million,” non-GAAP gross margin to “between 46% and 48%,” non-GAAP operating expenses to “between $106 million and $111 million,” and adjusted EBITDA loss to “between $40 million and $50 million.”
  • Brenton described quarterly cadence as: “revenue is expected to be modest in the first half, ramping in the second half, with the first quarter typically the lightest and the fourth quarter the strongest.”
  • Versus the prior quarter’s guidance framework, the company moved from Q4-only guidance to annual guidance, with Stehlin stating: “we believe annual financial guidance remains the most appropriate framework for setting expectations.”

Financial Results

  • With no usable analysts estimates provided, the transcript did not include a comparison to Street expectations.
  • Brenton reported Q4 revenue of $35.3 million and said it represented “approximately 31%” sequential growth from $26.9 million in Q3 2025; he attributed the year-over-year increase primarily to Markforged, which “contributed $20.7 million.”
  • Brenton reported Q4 gross profit of $17.6 million and adjusted gross margin of “approximately 49.7%,” and said sequential gross profit increased “approximately 38%” from $12.7 million in Q3.
  • Brenton said Q4 operating expenses were $27.3 million, “declined over 6%” sequentially from $29.2 million, and were “more than 16% relative to the previously identified baseline of approximately $32.5 million.”
  • Brenton reported Q4 adjusted EBITDA was “a loss of $9.8 million,” compared to “a loss of $16.6 million in the third quarter of 2025.”
  • For full-year 2025, Brenton reported revenue of $102.4 million, gross profit of $48.1 million with adjusted gross margin of “approximately 46.9%,” operating expenses of $101 million, and adjusted EBITDA “a loss of $53.2 million.”
  • Brenton said total cash, cash equivalents, deposits and marketable equity securities were “approximately $459.6 million” at December 31, 2025, down from “about $515.5 million” at the end of Q3, and said the change included “$19.8 million of cash used for share repurchases” and “$24.4 million related to changes in the fair value of marketable equity securities.”

Q&A

  • Moshe Sarsari, (indiscernible): “revenue at Markforged is actually down compared to 2024... How does that reconcile with what I just heard about continued momentum?” CEO Stehlin responded that year-over-year growth cited was “comparing the consolidated business now after the acquisition with the prior year,” and said the growth discussed for Markforged in Q4 was “the sequential growth in Q4 over Q3.”
  • Moshe Sarsari, (indiscernible): challenged the press release framing, saying it looked like “an attempt to mislead the readers.” CEO Stehlin replied: “it's definitely not an attempt to mislead,” adding that deeper in the press release the company “described the various comparisons, both year-over-year with and without Markforged.”
  • Moshe Sarsari, (indiscernible): asked, “Why are you discontinuing” share repurchases. CEO Stehlin said: “we think that there are better uses for our money at this point, which will become very clear in the second quarter,” and added: “We have not taken it off the table. It's still an option. We're just not going to talk about it in advance.”
  • Moshe Sarsari, (indiscernible): pressed for more specifics, calling management’s answer “very abstract.” CEO Stehlin responded: “you'll learn a whole lot more in this next quarter.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was negative and pressing, including allegations around disclosure and organic growth framing, such as “it's very not encouraging,” and “there is no explanation here.”
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive in Q&A, including “it's definitely not an attempt to mislead,” and repeated references to forthcoming disclosures: “in Q2, things should become a whole lot more clear.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter—where “there are no questions” and prepared remarks emphasized early operational changes—this quarter showed more adversarial tone driven by a direct challenge on revenue presentation and capital allocation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance evolved from Q3’s Q4 revenue range of $31.5 million to $33.5 million to Q4’s full-year 2026 framework of $130 million to $140 million revenue, 46% to 48% gross margin, $106 million to $111 million OpEx, and (-$40 million) to (-$50 million) adjusted EBITDA.
  • The strategic alternatives review shifted from Q3’s “no time line has been set” language to a more time-bound statement in Q4: “we expect that in the second quarter, we will make a series of announcements.”
  • Capital allocation messaging changed from Q3 highlighting buybacks because shares were “significantly undervalued” to Q4 stating the board is “carefully evaluating capital deployment priorities,” and Stehlin saying there may be “better uses” for cash.
  • Analyst focus changed from no Q3 Q&A to Q4 scrutiny of acquisition-driven growth, organic momentum, and the rationale for pausing buyback commentary.

Risks and Concerns

  • Stehlin cited a 2025 disruption: “the subsequent bankruptcy of one of the two acquisitions completed during that period,” and said the company “responded decisively in the second half of the year” by narrowing focus and executing with greater discipline.
  • Stehlin said “cautious capital spending continues to create variability in certain sectors,” despite noting tariff uncertainty had eased.
  • Stehlin disclosed internal control risk: “we identified a material weakness in internal control over financial reporting,” tied to “resource limitations impacting accounting for and disclosure of business combinations and related valuation analysis,” while also stating: “we have not identified any errors in previously issued financial statements do not expect any restatements.”
  • Brenton reiterated quarterly variability risk from order timing: “larger strategic orders... can create some variability in quarterly results,” adding: “this variability reflects timing differences rather than lost revenue.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q4 2025 as a quarter of improved execution and cost discipline, supported by $35.3 million in revenue, a 49.7% adjusted gross margin, and an adjusted EBITDA loss of $9.8 million, alongside a year-end liquidity position of $459.6 million. For 2026, the company issued annual guidance calling for $130 million to $140 million in revenue and an adjusted EBITDA loss of $40 million to $50 million, while repeatedly pointing investors to a planned set of Q2 announcements tied to its strategic alternatives review and capital deployment priorities, including reduced forward commentary on share repurchases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil prices pull back as hopes rise for end to Iran war; Trump says U.S. could leave in 2-3 weeks
data.description:
Crude oil futures closed lower Tuesday on rising hopes for an end to the war in the Middle East after The Wall Street Journal reported that President Trump would be willing to wind up military operations against Iran even before the Strait of Hormuz is reopened, followed by unconfirmed reports that Iran's President Pezeshkian said the country could end the war with certain guarantees.

After hours, Trump told reporters at the White House that the U.S. could leave Iran within 2-3 weeks, suggesting the U.S. had largely accomplished its military goals and would leave it to other countries to resolve issues with the Strait of Hormuz.

Earlier in the day, a frustrated Trump took to social media to criticize European countries that had not supported the U.S. in the war, telling those facing fuel shortages to "Go get your oil!"

Trump said countries such as Britain that are running out of jet fuel because the Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed and refuse to aid the U.S. in its efforts should buy from the U.S. or they should "build up some delayed courage, go to the Strait, and just TAKE IT."

However, the U.S. is already producing record amounts of jet fuel, but has little capacity to increase exports, as U.S. refiners are producing 1.9M bbl/day of jet fuel, with ~300K left over for exports after domestic consumption, according to U.S. government data.

Even if the U.S. was able to boost jet fuel production, the increase would pale in comparison to the nearly 8M bbl of the fuel the world consumes daily.

While oil prices fell, the S&P 500 surged 2.9% and the Nasdaq Composite jumped 3.8% on speculation that the U.S. and Iran may be looking for a way out of the conflict.

"The oil market is going to dictate the end of this war," Spartan Capital's Peter Cardillo said in a note. "Trump knows that if oil prices go to $125 or $130 and stay there for a while, it's going to cause havoc for the economy."

A global oil supply deficit of ~8.75M bbl/day is expected to persist through April even if the war ends by mid-month, analysts at Société Générale said in a note. "Gulf producers must restore output—potentially back to pre-conflict levels—tankers must successfully move those barrels to global refining hubs, and, critically, refiners must be able to process the crude into usable products."

Restarting production is particularly complex, requiring safety checks, system repairs, and gradual well ramp-ups to avoid reservoir damage, and critical infrastructure such as compressors and treatment plants also must be brought back online, a process industry estimates suggest could take weeks to several months, the bank said.

On Tuesday, front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery fell 1.4% to $101.38/bbl, and most-active Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery dropped 2.6% to $104.63/bbl, while the May contract expired at $118.35, up 4.9% on the day and posting its highest settlement value since June 16, 2022.

U.S. crude soared in Q1 by $43.96/bbl, or 76.6%, its largest dollar gain on record and sharpest percentage increase since Q2 2020, while Brent jumped by $57.50/bbl, or 94.5%, its biggest dollar gain on record and largest percentage gain since Q3 1990; for the month of March, Nymex and Brent crude rose 51.3% and 63.3%, respectively.

Front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for May delivery fell 0.1% Tuesday and 21.7% for the quarter to $2.884/MMBtu; for March, U.S. gas gained 0.9%.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Gen Restaurant Group targets $215M-$225M 2026 revenue while projecting CPG run rate over $100M within 3 years
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: GEN Restaurant Group (GENK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Wook Kim said the quarter faced “a very challenging environment,” citing that “the majority of our customer base is Hispanic in many of our markets, and they have been put under extreme pressure through the immigration enforcement,” adding that “this significantly reduces our customer traffic,” and that “the increase in the fuel prices because of the war has reduced customer discretionary spending,” contributing to “a decrease in our same-store sales.”
  • CEO Kim highlighted development activity and early 2026 openings: “We opened 15 restaurants in 2025, including six located in South Korea for a total of 57 restaurants in operation,” and “in the first quarter of 2026, we opened two additional restaurants in Tucson, Arizona and Denton, Texas.”
  • CEO Kim described a portfolio action: “we have recently entered into a joint venture with Chubby Cattle International to partner on five of our non-performing restaurants,” with GEN owning 49% and Chubby Cattle 51%, and said, “This transaction creates a $4.5 million write-down, but will create five profitable restaurants that will generate strong EBITDA in the future for which we are entitled to 49% of the profits.”
  • CEO Kim outlined multiple initiatives: “We have adjusted our menu to streamline options,” “enhanced our incentive program with restaurant managers,” tested “new boba drinks as well as soju drinks,” began exploring “our new digital platform,” and said, “we recently launched our GEN loyalty program and are accepting cryptocurrency for payments,” alongside “our new enhanced e-commerce website” and “an AI program to create efficiencies and reduce corporate overhead.”
  • CEO Kim said the Costco program performance was strong: “During 2025, we sold approximately $29 million in gift card to Costco, which is 150% increase over last year.”
  • CEO Kim laid out the CPG expansion and targets, stating, “we had expanded our CPG business to over 800 locations,” projecting, “By the end of 2026, we are projected to have our CPG products in 1,500 to 2,000 locations,” and “carried to 7,000 to 8,000 locations by the end of 2027,” adding, “we believe we can achieve a run rate of over $100 million in annual revenue as soon as 3 years,” with “an EBITDA margin in the high teens.”
  • CFO Thomas Croal reported, “During the fourth quarter, we generated total revenue of $49.7 million,” and said same-store sales “dropping by 11.6% for the fourth quarter.”

Outlook

  • Management guided, “we're targeting full year revenues of $215 million to $225 million in 2026 and achieving restaurant-level adjusted EBITDA margins in the 15% to 15.5% range,” and added, “By the end of 2026, we anticipate being at an annual run rate approaching $250 million in revenue.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter call, CFO Croal’s Q3 2025 target was “full year revenue of $220 million to $225 million” and an “annual run rate of approximately $250 million of revenue when all our new restaurants are open,” while Q4 2025 guidance shifted to a 2026 revenue range and reiterated a similar run-rate framing.
  • On pricing actions not discussed as taken in the prior quarter, CFO Croal said, “we implemented a $1 price increase at the majority of our restaurants in the first quarter of 2026, which equates to about a 2.5% price increase overall.”

Financial Results

  • Total revenue was $49.7 million (Q4 2025) vs. $60.35 million (analysts’ revenue estimate).
  • CFO Croal said Q4 included “a net loss before income taxes of $12.5 million, which equated to $0.36 per diluted share,” and also reported, “adjusted net income, a non-GAAP measure, we had a net loss of $5 million or $0.09 per diluted share.”
  • CFO Croal detailed cost pressure: “Cost of goods sold as a percentage of company restaurant sales increased by 285 basis points to 36.9%,” and for the full year, “cost of goods sold as a percentage of revenue increased from 33% in 2024 to 34.7% in 2025.”
  • CFO Croal cited operating deleverage: “Occupancy expenses as a percentage of company restaurant sales increased by 253 basis points to 11.2%,” and “Other operating expenses as a percentage of company restaurant sales increased 261 basis points to 12.4%.”
  • CFO Croal said G&A rose year over year: “G&A, excluding stock-based compensation was $23 million in 2025 compared to $18.4 million in 2024,” and added, “due to our decreased new restaurant openings in 2026, we expect there to be a reduction in G&A as we move forward.”
  • CFO Croal reported profitability compression: “our restaurant level adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $3.9 million or 7.9% of total revenue,” and “Total adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter of 2025 was negative $2.7 million.”
  • Liquidity commentary included: “As of December 31, 2025, we had approximately $2.8 million in cash and cash equivalents,” and “We have the majority of our $20 million revolving credit facility available.”

Q&A

  • George Kelly, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked what retail contribution, comps, and openings were embedded in the $215 million to $225 million revenue guide; CFO Thomas Croal said, “we're working towards getting to a $20 million run rate by the end of this year,” and “we should be in the $10 million range in the retail for this year,” implying “the restaurants in $205 million range looking at the low end.”
  • George Kelly, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked about 2026 openings/closures; CEO Wook Kim said, “we haven't really contemplated on the closures,” and on openings, “we opened two, we have one -- five under construction, and that will be completed this year,” adding, “Maybe we'll squeeze in 1 or 2 more towards the end of the year or the beginning of '27.”
  • George Kelly, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked about near-term retail profitability and investments; CEO Kim said, “we don't anticipate a lot at all in terms of infrastructure cost,” calling capital needs “purely inventory,” and reiterated, “when we said it will be in the high teens, that accounts for all” discounts and “slotting fees.”
  • George Kelly, ROTH Capital Partners: Pressed on why management is confident in longer-term CPG expectations; CEO Kim said, “we have not had a single turndown,” and claimed retail performance indicators: “They don't keep products on the shelves if they don't have velocity,” and “our velocity is above their velocities,” adding it is “unusual… to have a hit rate of 100%.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to skeptical, focusing on the durability and math behind 2026 guidance and early-stage retail scaling, with repeated requests to “drill into” the revenue guide and justify long-term CPG assumptions.
  • Management tone was slightly negative on macro conditions but confident on strategic pivots, with CEO Kim asserting CPG momentum and CFO Croal framing 2026 as a year to slow openings and improve margins; management used confident phrasing such as “we're targeting” and “we are projected.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, Q4 prepared remarks were more explicit about demand pressure (immigration enforcement, fuel prices) and included a concrete portfolio action (Chubby Cattle JV), while Q3 messaging emphasized ongoing expansion and confidence in the restaurant model.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The current call added specific external demand drivers: CEO Kim said customers “have retracted and are very afraid to come out” due to “immigration enforcement,” and pointed to “increase in the fuel prices because of the war,” while the prior call discussed traffic softness tied to “tariff days” and “ICE crack down” but with less emphasis on fuel prices.
  • Strategy shifted toward a more defined mix change: Q4 included the Chubby Cattle JV and a clear statement that “In 2026, we have significantly slowed our new restaurant growth plans,” while Q3 described slowing as conditional: “we will consider slowing our growth plans for 2026” if conditions did not improve.
  • Guidance framing changed from Q3’s 2025 outlook (“$220 million to $225 million”) to Q4’s 2026 targets (“$215 million to $225 million”) while maintaining the restaurant-level adjusted EBITDA margin range of “15% to 15.5%.”
  • The analyst focus narrowed in Q4 to CPG contribution and assumptions behind guidance, whereas Q3 covered a wider range (South Korea unit economics, labor efficiency, premium menu impact, competitive intensity).

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Kim tied sales risk to enforcement and consumer behavior: “our customers have retracted and are very afraid to come out,” which he said “significantly reduces our customer traffic.”
  • CFO Croal highlighted cost inflation and mitigation via pricing: “As a result of the inflationary impact on our meat prices, we implemented a $1 price increase… about a 2.5% price increase overall.”
  • CFO Croal cited reliance on liquidity and borrowing capacity to fund near-term plans: “We anticipate using a portion of our revolving credit facility” for “limited new restaurants” and “grow our grocery store initiatives.”
  • CEO Kim described CPG scaling dependencies: “Introducing our products to grocery store chains takes time to set up in their IT systems,” and in Q&A he noted “There's a lot of insurance. There's a lot of setups. Once the setup is done then the cash flow… is very seamless.”

Final Takeaway

Management described a Q4 shaped by lower traffic and higher costs, while outlining a sharper pivot toward margin recovery and CPG-led growth. The company guided 2026 revenue of $215 million to $225 million with restaurant-level adjusted EBITDA margins of 15% to 15.5%, slowed restaurant development, and emphasized CPG expansion to over 800 locations with projections of 1,500 to 2,000 locations by end of 2026 and a run rate over $100 million in annual revenue “as soon as 3 years,” alongside a portfolio move to convert five underperforming restaurants into a Chubby Cattle JV structure.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: J.Jill forecasts $70M-$75M FY2026 adjusted EBITDA as it plans about $20M free cash flow and lifts dividend to $0.09
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: J.Jill, Inc. (JILL) Q4 2025

Management view

  • CEO Mary Coyne framed 2025 as “the beginning of a strategic evolution,” saying the company is using “testing and learning” to expand its customer file through “product evolution, enhancing the customer journey and improving the way we work as an organization.” She said Q4 “reinforced why this evolution is essential,” citing “an early assortment that did not resonate as hoped,” “earlier and deeper competitive holiday promotions,” and a direct customer “migrat[ing] towards the promotional end of the spectrum.”
  • Coyne said the 2026 plan centers on broadening appeal while retaining core attributes: “We’re expanding into new categories and modernizing our aesthetic to appeal to a broader customer base. But doing so in a way that upholds the quality, fit and values that our loyal customers expect.” She added, “This evolution will take time, and we should not expect the path to be linear.”
  • Coyne highlighted leadership additions: “We brought Courtney O’Connor on board in July as Chief Merchandising Officer” and “Viv Rettke in November as the company’s first-ever Chief Growth Officer to lead our e-commerce and AI initiatives.”
  • On product, Coyne pointed to Q4 tests and a more visible shift in 2026: “Our summer 2026 assortment, which will be introduced in Q2, will capture the first influence from our strengthened merchant and design team,” including “more newness with silhouettes and fabrics” and “the beginning stages of expansion into areas of accessories such as bags and belts.”
  • Webb positioned FY2026 as an “investment and measured transition,” saying, “This requires deliberate investments that will pressure near-term profitability, but position us for stronger performance in 2027 and beyond.” He added, “Our strong balance sheet and cash position provide flexibility to execute this strategic evolution while continuing to return capital to shareholders.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide EPS guidance in the transcript.
  • For Q1 FY2026, Mark Webb guided to sales down approximately 5% to 7% and comp sales down approximately 7% to 9%, with adjusted EBITDA of $15M to $17M; he said Q1 gross margin is expected to be down about 400 basis points vs. Q1 FY2025, reflecting tariff and strategy transition pressures.
  • For FY2026, Webb guided to sales down 2% to about flat and comp sales down 3% to down 1%, with adjusted EBITDA of $70M to $75M. He said the company expects FY2026 gross margins down about 50 basis points vs. FY2025, as first-half tariff headwinds are “partially offset by better full-price selling, lower promotions and lower year-over-year tariffs beginning in Q4.”
  • Webb laid out tariff assumptions and costs: “Total tariff load net of vendor offsets in 2026 will be about $15 million compared to about $7.5 million incurred in 2025,” adding that guidance “does not assume receipt of any refunds of tariffs paid to date.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s outlook language, Q4 added more detailed tariff-rate assumptions (10% after February 28 through end of Q1 receipts, then 15% for the rest of the year) and explicitly tied a back-half improvement to both product flow and tariff normalization.

Financial results

  • For Q4 FY2025, Webb reported total sales of $138.4M (down 3.1% year-over-year) and comp sales down 4.8%, with store sales down 9% and direct sales up 2.6% (driven by markdown sales that “benefited from ship-from-store capabilities”).
  • Webb reported Q4 gross profit of $87.3M and gross margin of 63.1% (down 320 basis points year-over-year), driven by “approximately $4.5 million of net tariff costs” and “deeper year-over-year discounting.”
  • Webb reported adjusted EBITDA of $7.2M (vs. $14.5M in the prior-year quarter) and adjusted EPS of (-$0.02) (vs. $0.32).
  • For FY2025, Webb reported free cash flow of $23.2M and adjusted EBITDA of $84.3M on sales of $596.5M, while noting gross margin of 68.7% “despite incurring approximately $7.5 million of incremental net tariff costs.” He also said the company refinanced its $75M term loan, “sav[ing] approximately $2 million in annualized cash interest expense,” and returned capital via $10.4M of share repurchases and approximately $5M in ordinary dividends.

Q&A

  • Jungwon Kim, TD Cowen: asked how much of Q1 softness is macro vs. internal factors and what changes are planned for Mother’s Day; CEO Mary Coyne said the “challenging start” is “amidst of very tough macro backdrop,” felt “more in our direct channel,” while “what we are seeing in stores” is “very encouraging,” and said Mother’s Day will feature adjusted timing of “catalog” and “digital marketing” “backed up by a product drop… in the 10 days before.”
  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: asked what specifically is changing in assortment, who the company wants to acquire, and what margin components are doing; CEO Coyne said the assortment is moving to “a more modern aesthetic” with “a very balanced approach,” and said the company is targeting retention, new, and reactivation, with a target audience of “45 to 65” and opportunity in “the middle of that range”; CFO Mark Webb said ocean rates look “more flat… maybe up a tiny bit,” and that carrier fuel surcharges have been reflected in guidance SG&A.
  • Corey Tarlowe, Jefferies: asked about Q4 monthly cadence and QTD trends plus the new planning system’s costs/benefits; Webb said “January was the strongest” and “heavily markdown driven,” while “quarter-to-date, we’ve seen a challenging start”; Coyne said the Anaplan MP&A project shifts from “a very manual Excel-based system” to “predictive AI forecasting” intended to improve “revenue and margin,” and Webb said CapEx priorities include “new stores” and the “more targeted” Anaplan project.
  • Anna Linscott, William Blair: asked what is different vs. last year behind back-half improvement and whether pricing increases are planned; Coyne pointed to a stronger, aligned team and ongoing test-and-learn across product and marketing, while Webb highlighted tariff structure shifting from a first-half headwind to “a small tailwind” in Q4 (assuming stated rates); Coyne said pricing will be “very measured” amid heightened promotion and direct-channel price sensitivity.
  • Ethan Saghi, BTIG: asked which categories worked/lagged; Coyne said Q4 strength came from “newness and novelty,” with softness in “repeat programs,” citing wins in “travel capsule,” “expanded categories in outerwear,” early “accessories,” and “cashmere,” and said Q2 is when fabric, silhouette, and category mix more meaningfully reflect evolution, while noting “our dress business turnaround.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, centering on Q1 softness, reliance on macro stabilization, cadence visibility, and tariff/margin sensitivity (e.g., Jungwon Kim, TD Cowen: “how much of the softness… is due to macro versus other factors?”; Anna Linscott, William Blair: “how much… depends on macro stabilization or improvement?”).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more cautious in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing non-linearity and near-term pressure while pointing to store resilience and planned improvements (e.g., CEO Mary Coyne: “we should not expect the path to be linear”; “we will be taking a very measured approach to pricing”).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management used more explicit caution around near-term profitability pressure (“will pressure near-term profitability”) and provided more granular tariff framing, while still reiterating confidence in “test-and-learn” and the strategic evolution.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current call shifted from Q3’s emphasis on a “solid third quarter” and early marketing tests to a Q4 focus on holiday underperformance, heavier markdown reliance, and a tougher price-sensitive direct consumer.
  • Guidance focus moved from Q3’s Q4 adjusted EBITDA range of $3M to $5M and full-year adjusted EBITDA of $80M to $82M to a FY2026 adjusted EBITDA range of $70M to $75M, with management explicitly describing FY2026 as a year of investment and transition.
  • Strategic priorities remained consistent (product evolution, customer journey/marketing mix, operational modernization), but Q4 added a concrete implementation timeline: Anaplan MP&A “on track to go live late in the second half of 2026” with “meaningful benefits… in 2027.”

Risks and concerns

  • Coyne cited demand and pricing pressure: “our direct customer continue to migrate towards the promotional end of the spectrum,” and said 2026 started with “continued price sensitivity, particularly in our direct channel.”
  • Webb highlighted policy and cost uncertainty: “the continued uncertainty in the consumer and geopolitical environment, the turbulent trade policy landscape,” and said tariff assumptions are “subject to any additional changes.”
  • Management’s mitigation emphasized inventory discipline and controlled investment pacing, with Webb stating unit purchases are “positioned down in the mid-single digits” and Coyne reiterating a “methodical test-and-learn approach” before scaling.

Final takeaway

Management described FY2026 as an investment-led transition year, guiding to sales down 2% to about flat and adjusted EBITDA of $70M to $75M while navigating a higher expected tariff load and a price-sensitive direct customer; leadership emphasized that Q2 assortments and rebalanced marketing are intended to improve performance into the back half, alongside continued store expansion of about 5 net new locations and ongoing shareholder returns including a raised $0.09 dividend.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nuvve outlines OMNIA partnership to deploy 1 GW+ Europe battery pipeline over next 24 months
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nuvve Holding Corp. (NVVE) Q4 2025

Management view

  • CEO Gregory Poilasne framed 2025 as “a transition year where we have been pivoting from vehicle-to-grid deployments to stationary storage,” while keeping “current activities in school bus and fleets.” He said the platform is built for both mobile and stationary batteries, citing “aggregation, second by second control and advanced stacking services, including behind-the-meter energy cost optimization, distribution grid support and ancillary services.”
  • CEO Poilasne said Nuvve is expanding AI usage to reduce costs and scale: “Nuvve has now moved on into a full end-to-end AI-based product development cycle and is currently integrating AI-based project management, sales support and finance functionalities in order to scale our business while we are reducing our cost base.”
  • CEO Poilasne highlighted Europe as the core of the pivot, calling the OMNIA Global partnership “absolutely transformative” and saying OMNIA “has developed a 1 gigawatt plus battery pipeline across multiple countries in Europe that will be deployed over the next 24 months.” He added that the partnership’s purpose is to deploy batteries “that will be owned by Nuvve,” and cited three announced projects: Sweden (50-megawatt/75-megawatt hour), Austria (40-megawatt/80-megawatt hour), and Romania (60-megawatt/120-megawatt hour), totaling 150 megawatts.
  • CEO Poilasne quantified potential project economics: “Compensation for such battery projects can vary between $250,000 per megawatt per year to more than $500,000 per megawatt per year.”
  • CFO David Robson said, “In the fourth quarter, we generated total revenues of $1.93 million compared to $1.79 million in the fourth quarter of 2024,” and attributed the change to “higher product sales and increased grant revenues,” partially offset by “the absence of management fees earned related to the Fresno EV infrastructure project.”

Outlook

  • Management’s outlook centered on execution of the stationary storage pivot. CEO Poilasne said, “We are confident that our pivot towards stationary storage was the right choice,” and added, “moving forward, our success is going through battery deployments, especially in Europe and Japan.”
  • CFO Robson pointed to a near-term focus on cost and project milestones: “As we look out to the next several quarters, we expect to see more developments from our Europe and Japan stationary battery projects. We also anticipate improvements in our cash burn resulting from the benefit of lower operating costs compared with last year.”
  • Compared with Q3, management shifted from describing early-stage development (including Denmark projects) to emphasizing ownership-driven deployment in Europe through OMNIA, plus an expanded Japan approach that includes sales, aggregation platform selection, and “tolling” rental-style structures.

Financial results

  • CFO Robson reported full-year revenue: “Total revenues year-to-date through December 31, 2025, were $4.79 million, which compares to $5.29 million for the prior year period,” again citing the lack of Fresno management fees as the primary driver.
  • CFO Robson reported margin changes: “Margins on products, services and grant revenues were 24.2% for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to 15.8% for the year ago period,” and said the quarter benefited from “a higher mix of grant revenues and improved pricing on product revenues.”
  • CFO Robson disclosed a major one-time charge tied to charger reliability: “we recognized a total inventory impairment charge of $3.47 million,” after determining certain 125-kilowatt V2G DC chargers “were not conforming to our commercial product reliability standards, and they would no longer be offered for sale domestically.”
  • CFO Robson highlighted operating expense reductions: “Operating costs, excluding cost of sales and inventory impairment was $3.7 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $5.9 million for the third quarter of 2025,” and said the decline was “primarily driven by lower payroll expenses.”
  • CFO Robson reported liquidity and capital actions: “We had approximately $5.5 million in cash as of December 31, 2025,” and said the increase versus December 2024 was “primarily the result of capital raised through the issuance of preferred stock and the exercise of warrants totaling $8.1 million,” plus “$0.9 million from the sale of its equity investment in DREEV.”
  • CFO Robson reported backlog contraction: “At December 31, 2025, our hardware and service backlog decreased to $3.3 million,” and said the decrease “primarily relates to the termination of the Fresno EV infrastructure project in early February 2026.”

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript; the operator stated, “Showing no questions, this will conclude our question-and-answer session.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst sentiment was implied by the lack of participation, with no questions raised in both Q4 and Q3.
  • Slightly positive management tone emphasized conviction and momentum in the pivot, with CEO Poilasne stating, “The future of Nuvve in the stationary battery space looks bright,” while also tying progress to cost reductions.
  • Compared with Q3, management’s prepared-remarks confidence increased around Europe, shifting from project development language to describing the OMNIA partnership as “transformative” and outlining a defined 24-month deployment window.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategy emphasis moved further toward stationary batteries in Q4, with new detail on OMNIA’s “1 gigawatt plus battery pipeline” and Nuvve’s intent to own deployed assets, versus Q3’s focus on developing three 2-megawatt Denmark projects and discussing financing partners.
  • Q4 introduced a major hardware quality issue and write-down: the $3.47 million inventory impairment on 125-kilowatt V2G DC chargers and a decision they “would no longer be offered for sale domestically,” a topic not raised in Q3.
  • Backlog direction changed materially, with Q4 explicitly linking the drop to “termination of the Fresno EV infrastructure project in early February 2026,” whereas Q3 reported $19 million backlog and discussed looking ahead to New Mexico and Japan developments.

Risks and concerns

  • CFO Robson said certain 125-kilowatt V2G DC chargers “were not conforming to our commercial product reliability standards,” leading to a $3.47 million impairment and domestic sales discontinuation; he added the units “will be used to support our business development efforts in Taiwan.”
  • CFO Robson cited revenue pressure from project-specific fees: service revenues were impacted by “the absence of management fees earned related to the Fresno EV infrastructure project,” and backlog fell after the Fresno termination.
  • CEO Poilasne said U.S. battery projects “don't seem to be moving as fast as projects in Europe and Japan,” signaling pacing risk by geography.

Final takeaway

Management characterized 2025 as a pivot year away from a V2G-led deployment model toward stationary storage, anchored by the OMNIA Global partnership in Europe with a “1 gigawatt plus” pipeline targeted for deployment over 24 months and three announced projects totaling 150 megawatts. Financially, the quarter included $1.93 million in revenue, improved margins versus the prior-year quarter, and substantially lower operating costs, but also a $3.47 million inventory impairment tied to discontinued domestic sales of certain V2G DC chargers and a backlog reduction tied to the termination of the Fresno EV infrastructure project.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AuthID outlines over $30M pipeline while expecting RPO growth to resume in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: authID (AUID) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Rhoniel Daguro framed demand around “the rise of deepfakes to trick existing authentication systems” and “the rise of rogue AI agents accessing systems without human accountability and without human control,” adding, “they are calling us” and “these are serious advanced engagements with organizations that do not waste their time.”
  • Daguro highlighted customer/partner traction disclosed during 2025, including “a full production agreement with one of the top 20 retailers in the world,” emphasizing, “This is not a pilot. This is not a proof of concept. This is a live production deployment of our PrivacyKey technology,” with “a contractual pathway to expand this deployment into their retail stores worldwide.”
  • Daguro described channel/partner leverage, saying, “We announced a partnership with MajorKey Technologies,” and “MajorKey launched IDProof+, a product built in direct collaboration with authID,” and added on NEC’s subsidiary, “We continue to expand our partnership with NESIC,” noting “Phase 1… is live” and that “together, we launched IDX.”
  • Daguro positioned Agentic AI governance as a core strategic wedge: “in November 2025, we unveiled the authID Mandate framework,” adding, “It binds every AI agent to a verified human sponsor using biometric anchored identity… and produces a tamper-evident audit trail for every action.” He also said, “in November, we were accepted in the NVIDIA Connect program.”
  • CFO Ed Sellitto summarized Q4 results: “Total revenue for the quarter was $0.4 million compared to $0.2 million last year,” and added, “Operating expenses for Q4 were $4.5 million, down from $4.9 million last year.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide explicit revenue or EPS guidance figures in the prepared remarks.
  • Sellitto signaled an RPO direction change into 2026: “We believe the RPO reductions from our earlier contracts are now fully factored in… and stability in our RPO going forward,” and “We expect to resume RPO growth in 2026 as we gain traction closing additional enterprise deals in our pipeline in the coming months.”
  • Daguro described the demand backdrop and pipeline size: “the market demand for authID is high,” and “we have a pipeline of over $30 million in active engagements with a significant number of large enterprise accounts,” while noting, “It takes time to close these deals.”
  • Compared with Q3, management again cited lengthening enterprise cycles; Daguro said Q4 full-year “gross bARR of $2.4 million came in below the $6 million revised target that I said on the Q3 call due to the sales cycles on these enterprise deals being longer than we modeled.”

Financial Results

  • Sellitto reported full-year revenue and expense levels: “For the year, total revenue was $2.0 million compared with $0.9 million a year ago, representing a year-over-year increase of 129%,” while “for the full year, operating expenses were $20.2 million compared with $15.6 million in 2024.”
  • Profitability and per-share results included: “Net loss for the quarter was $4.0 million,” and “Our net loss per share for the quarter improved to $0.28 compared with $0.42 a year ago,” while “for the full year, net loss was $17.9 million,” and “net loss per share improved slightly to $1.38 compared with $1.40 last year.”
  • Contract-related impacts remained central. Daguro said, “Two early large engagements underperformed,” and “The combined concessions for the full year were approximately $884,000,” adding, “we are not recognizing further revenue from either contract until we reach resolution on revised terms.”
  • RPO and quarterly movement were a key swing factor: “As of December 31, 2025, our total RPO was $2.2 million, a decrease of $1.4 million versus last quarter due to a reduction of contracted minimum fees related to a customer with delayed growth in their business.”
  • Bookings and recurring metrics showed mixed signals: Sellitto said “ARR as of Q4 is $1.8 million compared to $1.7 million of ARR as of Q3,” while “the gross amount of bARR signed in the fourth quarter of 2025 was $0.1 million, down from $7.1 million of gross bARR a year ago.”

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: No analyst questions were included, so analyst tone was not observable in the current quarter transcript.
  • Management: Slightly positive in tone, with Daguro calling the period “a pivotal moment for authID,” while also acknowledging friction in deal timing: “It takes time to close these deals.”
  • Versus Q3: The Q4 prepared remarks leaned more toward enterprise-demand emphasis (deepfakes/AI agents and platform distribution), while Q3 included more detail on “negative net revenue” dynamics and customer-specific revenue recognition actions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 added new distribution and platform references—Daguro said, “in January, we announced our integration with ServiceNow, adding authID to the ServiceNow Store,” and pointed to reach across “8,400 contact centers worldwide, including 85% of Fortune 500 companies”—while Q3 centered more on pipeline descriptions and the revised “$6 million” bARR target.
  • Q4 reiterated the same root driver behind the revised bARR expectation: Daguro attributed missing the revised target to “sales cycles… being longer than we modeled,” consistent with Q3 commentary that enterprise cycles were longer.
  • Financially, Q4 provided full-year rollups (including “$2.0 million” revenue and “$17.9 million” net loss) and disclosed a sharper RPO reference point (“$2.2 million” at year-end) versus Q3’s quarter-end RPO (“$3.6 million”).
  • Participation changed: Q3 included “Founder and CTO, Tom Szoke,” while Q4’s speakers were Daguro and Sellitto (plus legal), with no CTO remarks in the prepared section.

Risks and Concerns

  • Daguro said two earlier large engagements “underperformed,” and that the company is “not recognizing further revenue from either contract until we reach resolution on revised terms.”
  • Sellitto flagged contract minimum-fee pressure in RPO: the quarter-end RPO decline was “due to a reduction of contracted minimum fees related to a customer with delayed growth in their business.”
  • Both executives emphasized long enterprise sales cycles as a continuing headwind; Sellitto said, “the time line for these larger enterprise deals continues to draw out longer than expected.”

Final Takeaway

authID’s Q4 and full-year narrative centered on rising inbound enterprise attention tied to deepfakes and Agentic AI accountability, alongside concrete partner-led distribution (including ServiceNow store availability) and named 2025 wins such as a live, production PrivacyKey deployment at a top-20 global retailer. Management simultaneously underscored that two earlier large engagements remain unresolved—limiting near-term revenue recognition—and highlighted that longer enterprise sales cycles weighed on 2025 bookings, while pointing to “over $30 million” of active engagements and an expectation to “resume RPO growth in 2026” as pipeline deals close.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Microsoft in talks with Chevron, Engine No. 1 about $7B Texas power plant - Bloomberg
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) is in exclusive talks with Chevron (CVX) and investment fund Engine No. 1 over a long-term deal that would underpin a massive energy complex in West Texas, providing electricity to power a large data center campus, Bloomberg reported after-hours Tuesday.

The proposed natural gas-fired power plant is projected to cost ~$7B and initially generate 2,500 MW of electricity, making it one of the largest of its kind in the U.S., according to the report.

Chevron (CVX) previously disclosed some details regarding its proposed power plant—such as the chosen site near the city of Pecos, close to the Texas-New Mexico border in the heart of the oil-producing Permian Basin—but not the end user.

The Permian produces so much natural gas—a by-product of oil—that it often overwhelms pipelines, so some gas is burned off because it cannot be transported, making the region an ideal location for power plants.

A deal with Microsoft (MSFT) would secure a long-term customer for the plant's electricity and help finance its construction.

The project, which could be up and running before 2030, still requires tax and environmental approvals as well as agreement on commercial terms.
